---
title: "L-Infinity Cells and Truncated CSTs: A Quick Introduction"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{L-Infinity Cells and Truncated CSTs: A Quick Introduction}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
library(linf)
set.seed(1)
```

## Overview

This vignette shows the core workflow:

1. Normalize rows with `normalize.linf()`.
2. Assign dominant-feature labels with `linf.cells()`.
3. Build truncated CSTs with `linf.csts()`.

## L-Infinity normalization

```{r}
S.counts <- matrix(
  rpois(30, lambda = 5),
  nrow = 10,
  ncol = 3,
  dimnames = list(paste0("s", 1:10), paste0("t", 1:3))
)

Z <- normalize.linf(S.counts)
apply(Z, 1, max)
```

## L-Infinity cells

```{r}
colnames(Z) <- c("Lactobacillus", "Gardnerella", "Anaerobes")
cells <- linf.cells(Z)

str(cells)
cells$observed.levels
```

## Truncated CSTs

```{r}
A <- matrix(c(5, 1, 0), nrow = 6, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
B <- matrix(c(1, 5, 0), nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
C <- matrix(c(1, 0, 5), nrow = 2, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
S <- rbind(A, B, C)
S <- sweep(S, 1, rowSums(S), "/")
colnames(S) <- c("Dom1", "Dom2", "Dom3")

res <- linf.csts(S, n0 = 5)
table(res$cell.label, useNA = "ifany")
```

## Count-to-CST pipeline

```{r}
S <- matrix(rpois(100, lambda = 5), nrow = 20, ncol = 5)
res <- asv.to.linf.csts(S, min.lib = 10, prev.prop = 0.1, min.count = 1)
names(res)
```
